decks.name: Line Symmetry and Mirror Images
decks.description: Learn what symmetry means and how mirror images work

cards.back:
Symmetry means that one half of a shape is the exact mirror image of the other half.

If you fold a butterfly along the middle, both wings match perfectly. That is symmetry!
cards.hint: Think of a butterfly with both wings looking the same

cards.front: What is a line of symmetry?
cards.back:
A line of symmetry is an imaginary line that divides a shape into two equal halves that are mirror images of each other.

It is like a fold line - if you fold the shape along this line, both halves overlap perfectly.
cards.hint: Think of folding a paper so both halves match exactly

cards.back:
A mirror image is what you see when you look at something in a mirror. The left side appears on the right and the right side appears on the left.

If you raise your right hand, your mirror image raises its left hand!
cards.hint: Stand in front of a mirror and wave your right hand

cards.front: How can you check if a shape has a line of symmetry?
cards.back:
Fold the shape along the line. If both halves match exactly when folded, the line is a line of symmetry.

You can also place a mirror along the line. If the reflection completes the shape perfectly, it is symmetric.
cards.hint: Try the folding test - fold and see if both halves overlap

cards.front: Can a shape have more than one line of symmetry?
cards.back:
Yes! Some shapes have many lines of symmetry.

A square has 4 lines of symmetry.
A circle has unlimited lines of symmetry.
A rectangle has 2 lines of symmetry.
A heart shape has only 1 line of symmetry.
cards.hint: Think about a square - you can fold it in several ways and both halves match

decks.name: Symmetrical Shapes Around Us
decks.description: Discover symmetry in everyday objects and nature

cards.front: Name 3 things in nature that have symmetry.
cards.back:
1. A butterfly - both wings are mirror images
2. A leaf - the left and right halves match along the midrib
3. A human face - the left side roughly mirrors the right side

Nature is full of symmetry!
cards.hint: Look at insects, plants, and even your own body

cards.front: Which English letters have a line of symmetry?
cards.back:
Letters with a vertical line of symmetry: A, M, T, U, V, W, Y
Letters with a horizontal line of symmetry: B, C, D, E, K
Letters with both: H, I, O, X

Letters like F, G, J, L, N have no line of symmetry.
cards.hint: Write the letter and try folding it in half

cards.front: Does a rangoli design have symmetry?
cards.back:
Yes! Most rangoli designs are symmetrical. They often have many lines of symmetry.

When you draw a rangoli, you usually make the same pattern on all sides. This makes it look beautiful and balanced.
cards.hint: Think of the rangoli patterns you see during Diwali

cards.back: Not always! An equilateral triangle (all sides equal) has 3 lines of symmetry. An isosceles triangle (two sides equal) has 1 line of symmetry. But a scalene triangle (all sides different) has no line of symmetry at all.
cards.hint: It depends on the type of triangle

cards.front: Name some everyday objects that are symmetrical.
cards.back:
Symmetrical objects around us:
- A cricket bat (front view)
- Taj Mahal (from the front)
- A kite
- Spectacles
- A flower like a sunflower
- Traffic signs like the red octagon (stop sign)
cards.hint: Look at objects that have matching left and right sides

decks.name: Folding, Cutting, and Completing Figures
decks.description: Learn how to use folding and cutting to create symmetric shapes and complete figures

cards.front: How can you make a symmetrical shape by folding and cutting?
cards.back:
1. Take a piece of paper and fold it in half.
2. Draw half of a shape along the fold line.
3. Cut along the line you drew.
4. Open the paper - you get a perfectly symmetrical shape!

The fold line becomes the line of symmetry.
cards.hint: This is how we make paper decorations and snowflakes

cards.front: How do you complete a symmetric figure?
cards.back:
If you have half a shape and a line of symmetry, draw the mirror image on the other side.

For each point on the given half, mark a matching point at the same distance on the other side of the line. Connect the points to complete the shape.
cards.hint: Each point should be the same distance from the line of symmetry on both sides

cards.front: If you fold a paper twice and cut a small triangle, how many triangles will you see when you open it?
cards.back:
You will see 4 triangles! Each fold doubles the number of cut shapes.

1 fold and cut = 2 shapes
2 folds and cut = 4 shapes

This is why paper snowflakes look so beautiful - many folds create many symmetric patterns!
cards.hint: Each fold doubles the cuts you make

cards.front: What happens when you place a mirror on the line of symmetry of half a shape?
cards.back:
The mirror shows the reflection of the half shape. Together with the original half, it looks like the complete symmetrical shape!

This is why a line of symmetry is also called a mirror line.
cards.hint: The mirror creates the missing half

cards.front: How is symmetry used in real life?
cards.back:
Symmetry is used in many ways:
- Architects use it to design buildings like the Taj Mahal
- Artists use it in rangoli and mehndi designs
- Tailors use it to cut cloth for shirts (left and right sides match)
- Engineers use it to build bridges and vehicles
- Nature uses it in flowers, leaves, and butterflies
cards.hint: Think about designs, buildings, and nature
